content.text: We now study equations of the form $ax \equiv b \pmod{n}$.

content.title: Linear Congruence Theorem
content.content:
The congruence $ax \equiv b \pmod{n}$ has a solution if and only if 
$d \mid b$, where $d = \gcd(a, n)$.

When solutions exist, there are exactly $d$ incongruent solutions modulo $n$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The congruence $ax \equiv b \pmod{n}$ is equivalent to $ax = b + ny$ for 
some integer $y$, i.e., $ax - ny = b$. By the theory of linear Diophantine 
equations, this has solutions iff $d \mid b$.

When $d \mid b$, dividing by $d$ gives $(a/d)x \equiv (b/d) \pmod{n/d}$. 
Since $\gcd(a/d, n/d) = 1$, there is a unique solution $x_0$ modulo $n/d$. 
The $d$ incongruent solutions modulo $n$ are 
$x_0, x_0 + n/d, x_0 + 2n/d, \ldots, x_0 + (d-1)n/d$.

content.title: Solving a Linear Congruence
content.solution:
$\gcd(15, 24) = 3$ and $3 \mid 9$, so solutions exist.

Dividing by 3: $5x \equiv 3 \pmod{8}$.

Since $\gcd(5, 8) = 1$, we need the inverse of 5 modulo 8. 
Since $5 \cdot 5 = 25 \equiv 1 \pmod{8}$, the inverse is 5.

$x \equiv 5 \cdot 3 \equiv 15 \equiv 7 \pmod{8}$.

The three solutions modulo 24 are $x \equiv 7, 15, 23 \pmod{24}$.

content.title: Modular Inverse
content.content:
An integer $a^{-1}$ is the **modular inverse** of $a$ modulo $n$ if 
$a \cdot a^{-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$.

content.title: Existence of Inverses
content.content:
The integer $a$ has a modular inverse modulo $n$ if and only if 
$\gcd(a, n) = 1$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
The congruence $ax \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ has a solution iff $\gcd(a, n) \mid 1$, 
i.e., iff $\gcd(a, n) = 1$.

content.text:
The inverse can be computed using the extended Euclidean algorithm: find 
$x, y$ with $ax + ny = 1$, then $a^{-1} \equiv x \pmod{n}$.

content.title: Computing an Inverse
content.solution:
Using the extended Euclidean algorithm:
$30 = 7 \cdot 4 + 2$, $7 = 2 \cdot 3 + 1$, so $1 = 7 - 2 \cdot 3 
= 7 - (30 - 7 \cdot 4) \cdot 3 = 7 \cdot 13 - 30 \cdot 3$.

Therefore $7^{-1} \equiv 13 \pmod{30}$. Check: $7 \cdot 13 = 91 = 90 + 1 \equiv 1$.

content.title: Units Modulo $n$
content.content:
The set of **units** modulo $n$ is
\[
(\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^* = \{[a] : \gcd(a, n) = 1\}.
\]
This set forms a group under multiplication.
